key_points: Frat houses described as literal shitholes by visitors
Transfer housing is a concern — few direct answers available
On-campus options exist but quality varies significantly
key_quotes: Those frat houses were 💩holes 💀. I lived in something similar when I was a senior in Topeka Kansas
She's a freshman transfer spring 2023. What's the best dorms and is it easy for transfers to find one

key_points: Party/Greek scene exists but frat housing quality is rough
Campus described as having good work-life balance by applicants
Newark town scene limited — Grottos pizza mislabeled as a bar
key_quotes: I love how UD seems to have a good balance between work and fun
Grottos isn't really a bar. It's a pizza place. It's like calling Applebee's a bar

key_points: No substantive dining data from either source
Grottos Pizza is a local off-campus staple students mention
Campus food culture largely undocumented in student reviews
key_quotes: Grottos isn't really a bar. It's a pizza place. It's like calling Applebee's a bar. It's still awesome though

key_points: No direct mentions of counseling or mental health resources
Students describe stress around admissions deferrals and waitlists
One student dropped out citing 'suffering grades' and mental strain
key_quotes: I was originally going to University of Delaware, but dropped out due to suffering grades
I currently have a 3.725 cumulative GPA and want to transfer to college for a better experience... I want a more academically competitive school

key_points: OOS tuition hits $45-60k/year with little to no aid for many
Merit scholarships are transparent and stat-based — easy to calculate
Financial aid office accused of fabricating scholarship offers
key_quotes: I'm currently attending the University of Delaware — I got no scholarships so I'm paying $45K a year (out of state)

I got an email telling me I received a scholarship. However, I couldn't login to my account... he told me there was no scholarship listed THEN he tried to talk me into taking out 52k in loans per year
Was accepted to UD with full ride merit scholarship. Cornell wants us to pay $61k a year

key_points: Chemical Engineering ranked 6th-7th nationally — legitimately elite
ChemE and chemistry are standout programs with strong peer reputation
CS ranked around 70th nationally — solid but not exceptional
key_quotes: UDel was ranked 6th in the country for ChemE this past year
US News & World Report has ranked Delaware ChBE as the 7th best chemical engineering undergraduate program in the US
UMD is ranked ~15th in the nation in regards to [CS], versus UDel's rank of 70

key_points: Financial aid office gave misleading scholarship info to at least one student
Portal and SRAR system caused missed EA deadlines for applicants
Scholarship notification delays of 6+ weeks reported post-acceptance
key_quotes: He told me there was no scholarship listed THEN he tried to talk me into taking out 52k in loans per year to attend by telling me about UD's reputation
Totally forgot to set up my U Delaware portal and didn't see that they require submitting the SRAR as well. Now it looks like I went from early action to regular decision
It's been like 6 weeks now and I still haven't heard back from the school about the scholarships

key_points: Newark, DE is quiet/suburban — not an urban college town
Campus itself is considered attractive and walkable by visitors
Delaware as a state is routinely forgotten — 'a rest stop on I-95'
key_quotes: Delaware is a rest stop on rt 95 between Philly and Baltimore
I only remember Delaware because Biden was born there
I quite loved University of Delaware's campus

key_points: ChemE grads have strong outcomes given program's national ranking
Business/finance networking praised by some, questioned vs Penn State
Trust management minor guarantees internship at Delaware trust companies
key_quotes: Go to UDEL, work hard and get a job, if not, go to Cornell for grad school
The minor is new and only has 12 people in it and we are all guaranteed an internship at a trust company in Delaware
I hear a lot of good things about Penn State, such as networking, their finance program

key_points: In-state tuition is strong value, especially for ChemE/sciences
OOS without merit aid ($45-60k/year) is widely considered a bad deal
Full-ride merit scholarships exist and are worth aggressively pursuing
key_quotes: UD being 60k a year... we will most likely have to take out some loans, which is the main drawback
I'm currently attending the University of Delaware — I got no scholarships so I'm paying $45K a year (out of state). I'm realizing I don't want to go into debt
Because it's ChemE at UDel, and full ride, UDEL!

key_points: Safe, suburban, laid-back — not a high-energy urban campus
Strong academic pockets (ChemE, chemistry) alongside average ones
Genuinely liked by students who chose it deliberately, not as fallback
key_quotes: Although this was my safety I feel SO relieved to know I'm in somewhere, especially somewhere I'd be perfectly happy going to!
I love how UD seems to have a good balance between work and fun
i've visited campus a couple times already and everybody has been sooooo nice
